name: Regex tokens
description: Regular-expression syntax, token by token — character classes, anchors, quantifiers, groups, and lookarounds. Term: the token; definition: what it matches and how it behaves. Based on the common PCRE / Perl-style flavour used in most languages.
primer:
Tokens in the common PCRE / Perl-style flavour. Most metacharacters lose their special meaning inside `[...]`.

## Character classes (match one character)

- `.` — any single character *except newline* (matches newline too under the dot-all / single-line flag).
- `\d` — a digit `0-9`; negation `\D` is any non-digit.
- `\w` — a *word character*: letter, digit, or underscore; negation `\W`.
- `\s` — any whitespace (space, tab, newline, carriage return, form feed); negation `\S`.
- `[abc]` — one character from the set {a, b, c}.
- `[^abc]` — one character *not* in the set (leading `^` inside brackets negates).
- `[a-z]` — one character in a *range*; combinable, e.g. `[a-zA-Z0-9]`.

## Anchors & boundaries (zero-width — match a position, consume nothing)

- `^` — start of string (or start of line in multiline mode).
- `$` — end of string (or end of line in multiline mode).
- `\b` — word boundary: the position between a `\w` and a non-word char (or string edge); used to match whole words.
- `\B` — *not* a word boundary (between two word chars, or two non-word chars).

## Quantifiers (repeat the preceding token; greedy by default = match as much as possible)

- `*` — zero or more.
- `+` — one or more (fails if absent).
- `?` — zero or one (makes the token *optional*). After a quantifier it instead makes it *lazy*.
- `{n,m}` — between n and m times inclusive; `{n}` exactly n; `{n,}` n or more.
- `*?` — *lazy* (non-greedy) zero-or-more: match as *few* characters as possible. The trailing `?` makes any quantifier lazy — useful to stop a match before the first delimiter.

## Groups & lookarounds

- `(abc)` — *capturing* group: matches the sequence and saves it in a numbered capture (`\1`, etc.).
- `(?:abc)` — *non-capturing* group: groups for quantifier/alternation but saves nothing; cheaper, keeps capture numbering clean.
- `a|b` — *alternation*: match pattern a or b; low precedence, so it splits at the widest scope unless a group constrains it.
- `(?=...)` — *positive lookahead*: zero-width assertion that the following text matches, without consuming it. E.g. `\d+(?= dollars)`.
- `(?!...)` — *negative lookahead*: zero-width assertion that the following text does *not* match. E.g. `foo(?!bar)`.

cards.definition: Matches any single character except a newline (by default). With the dot-all / single-line flag, it also matches newlines.
Matches any single digit, 0-9. Its negation is \D, which matches any non-digit character.
Matches any word character: a letter, a digit, or an underscore. Its negation \W matches any non-word character.
cards.summary: A word char: letter, digit, or underscore.

cards.definition: Matches any whitespace character — space, tab, newline, carriage return, form feed. Its negation \S matches any non-whitespace character.
A character class: matches a single character that is one of a, b, or c. Inside brackets most metacharacters lose their special meaning.
A negated character class: matches a single character that is NOT a, b, or c. The leading caret inside the brackets negates the set.
A range inside a character class: matches any single lowercase letter from a to z. Ranges can be combined, e.g. [a-zA-Z0-9].
cards.summary: One character in the range a to z.

cards.definition: An anchor matching the start of the string (or the start of a line in multiline mode). It matches a position, not a character.
An anchor matching the end of the string (or the end of a line in multiline mode). It matches a position, consuming no characters.
A word-boundary anchor: matches the zero-width position between a word character (\w) and a non-word character (or string edge). Used to match whole words.
A non-word-boundary anchor: matches any position that is NOT a word boundary — i.e. between two word characters or between two non-word characters.
A quantifier meaning zero or more of the preceding token. Greedy by default — it matches as much as possible while still allowing the overall match to succeed.
A quantifier meaning one or more of the preceding token. Greedy by default. Fails if the token does not appear at least once.
A quantifier meaning zero or one of the preceding token — i.e. it makes the token optional. Placed after another quantifier (e.g. *?), it instead makes it lazy.
A quantifier matching the preceding token between n and m times inclusive. {n} matches exactly n; {n,} matches n or more.
A lazy (non-greedy) quantifier: zero or more, but matching as FEW characters as possible. The trailing ? makes any quantifier lazy. Useful to stop a match running past the first delimiter.
A capturing group: matches the sequence abc and saves the matched text in a numbered capture, referenceable later as \1 or in the result.
A non-capturing group: groups abc for applying a quantifier or alternation, but does NOT save the match into a capture slot. Cheaper and keeps capture numbering clean.
Alternation: matches either the pattern a or the pattern b. It has low precedence, so it splits at the widest scope unless constrained by a group.
A positive lookahead: a zero-width assertion that the following text matches the pattern, without consuming it. E.g. \d+(?= dollars) matches digits only if followed by ' dollars'.
A negative lookahead: a zero-width assertion that the following text does NOT match the pattern, without consuming it. E.g. foo(?!bar) matches 'foo' not followed by 'bar'.
